title: BertNet: Harvesting Knowledge Graphs from Pretrained Language Models

structured.summary: This paper presents BertNet, a framework for extracting explicit symbolic knowledge graphs with arbitrary relations from frozen pretrained language models using minimal input prompts and example entity pairs. It introduces prompt paraphrasing and weighting, a consistency-based scoring function, and efficient entity pair search, demonstrating large-scale KG construction with diverse relations and competitive accuracy validated by human evaluation.

strengths.point: Novel framework enabling explicit symbolic KG extraction for arbitrary new relations from frozen LMs without external training
strengths.evidence: The method requires only minimal natural language prompts and few example pairs to harvest large-scale KGs covering hundreds of relations not previously covered.

strengths.point: Automatic prompt paraphrasing and weighting improves robustness and accuracy over single prompt approaches
strengths.evidence: Ablations show that multiple diverse prompts generated and weighted automatically outperform single human-written prompts on relation extraction accuracy.

strengths.point: Efficient search and pruning algorithm scales to large entity vocabularies and supports extension to n-ary relations
strengths.evidence: The pruning and re-ranking strategy allows tractable search in quadratic entity pair space, enabling extraction of large KGs.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation on diverse relation sets including ConceptNet, LAMA, user-defined, and automatically derived relations
strengths.evidence: Human evaluations indicate accuracy around 65% on hundreds of arbitrary relations, with analyses showing benefits of larger and better pretrained LMs.

strengths.point: Provides interpretable symbolic KG outputs that facilitate understanding LM knowledge capacity and enable new knowledge discovery
strengths.evidence: The harvested KGs serve as explicit, analyzable representations of implicit LM knowledge, with publicly released code and demo.

weaknesses.point: Lack of direct comparison to in-context prompting methods on large autoregressive LMs like GPT-3
weaknesses.evidence: No experiments compare BertNet’s symbolic extraction versus few-shot prompting on GPT-3 or similar models for the same arbitrary relations.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient methodological details and ablations on prompt filtering thresholds, pruning criteria, and sensitivity
weaknesses.evidence: The paper omits precise filtering thresholds for semantic similarity and diversity, pruning algorithm specifics, and impact of hyperparameter choices.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation scope with missing recall/coverage analyses and inter-annotator agreement in human evaluation
weaknesses.evidence: Human evaluation reports accuracy but not inter-annotator agreement or recall, leaving completeness and annotation reliability unclear.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility concerns due to unspecified paraphrasing models, LM API/tokenizer details, and example pair selection strategy
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not disclose which paraphrasing models or LM checkpoints were used, nor tokenizer normalization or example pair sampling methods.

weaknesses.point: Claims of extensibility to n-ary relations are not empirically validated or clearly illustrated
weaknesses.evidence: Only binary relation results are presented without concrete examples or experiments on ternary or higher-arity relation extraction.

weaknesses.point: Limited analysis on generalizability regarding example pair quantity/quality and paraphrasing model dependency
weaknesses.evidence: No systematic study on how the number or representativeness of example pairs or paraphrasing quality affects KG extraction robustness.

weaknesses.point: Presentation could be improved with clearer explanations and concrete examples of prompts, scoring, and terminology
weaknesses.evidence: Technical terms are used without intuitive definitions or illustrative examples, reducing accessibility for a broader audience.

questions.question: How does BertNet’s symbolic KG extraction compare in accuracy and coverage to large autoregressive LMs using in-context prompting on the same relations?
questions.motivation: Direct comparison would clarify whether symbolic extraction offers unique advantages or complements existing prompting methods.

questions.question: What is the impact of the number and representativeness of example entity pairs on the quality and coverage of the harvested KG?
questions.motivation: Understanding sensitivity to input examples is crucial for practical deployment and user guidance.

questions.question: How robust is the method to noise or lower quality in paraphrased prompts, and how do different paraphrasing models affect performance?
questions.motivation: Since the method depends heavily on prompt quality, robustness to paraphrasing errors affects generalizability.

questions.question: Can the method be effectively applied to domain-specific pretrained LMs and relations requiring complex reasoning beyond co-occurrence?
questions.motivation: Extending to specialized domains would broaden applicability and test method limits.

questions.question: What are the practical runtimes, scalability limits, and resource requirements of the pruning and search algorithm when scaling to very large vocabularies?
questions.motivation: Detailed efficiency analysis informs feasibility for real-world large-scale KG extraction.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of direct comparison to in-context prompting methods on large autoregressive LMs like GPT-3. No experiments compare BertNet’s symbolic extraction versus few-shot prompting on GPT-3 or similar models for the same arbitrary relations
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient methodological details and ablations on prompt filtering thresholds, pruning criteria, and sensitivity. The paper omits precise filtering thresholds for semantic similarity and diversity, pruning algorithm specifics, and impact of hyperparameter choices
critique_points.point_003: Limited evaluation scope with missing recall/coverage analyses and inter-annotator agreement in human evaluation. Human evaluation reports accuracy but not inter-annotator agreement or recall, leaving completeness and annotation reliability unclear
critique_points.point_004: Reproducibility concerns due to unspecified paraphrasing models, LM API/tokenizer details, and example pair selection strategy. The paper does not disclose which paraphrasing models or LM checkpoints were used, nor tokenizer normalization or example pair sampling methods
critique_points.point_005: Claims of extensibility to n-ary relations are not empirically validated or clearly illustrated. Only binary relation results are presented without concrete examples or experiments on ternary or higher-arity relation extraction
critique_points.point_006: Limited analysis on generalizability regarding example pair quantity/quality and paraphrasing model dependency. No systematic study on how the number or representativeness of example pairs or paraphrasing quality affects KG extraction robustness
critique_points.point_007: Presentation could be improved with clearer explanations and concrete examples of prompts, scoring, and terminology. Technical terms are used without intuitive definitions or illustrative examples, reducing accessibility for a broader audience
